mnemonics.title: MBR Partition Limits
mnemonics.content: P.E.N.L - Primary (4), Extended (1), No more than (4), Logical (unlimited in extended)

mnemonics.title: Mount Options Order
mnemonics.content: R.W.N.S.D - Read, Write, Noexec, SUID, Dev (mnemonic for common mount flags: ro, rw, noexec, nosuid, nodev)

mnemonics.title: GPT Advantages
mnemonics.content: G.R.E.A.T - GUID based, Redundant table, Endless capacity (8ZB), Advanced partitioning, Two (128) partitions

acronyms.usage: Legacy partition scheme in first sector of disk; contains boot code and partition table
Modern partitioning standard using Globally Unique Identifiers; part of UEFI specification
Method of addressing disk sectors sequentially; 32-bit in MBR, 64-bit in GPT
Kernel abstraction layer providing uniform interface for different file systems
Linux configuration file for automatic mount options at boot time

visualTips.visualization: Draw MBR as single block (512 bytes) with tiny partition table. Draw GPT as multiple blocks with primary header, partition entries, backup header - showing redundancy
Think of mounting like attaching a folder (mount point) to a filing cabinet drawer (partition). The drawer becomes accessible through that folder location
Show disk → Extended partition box → Multiple logical partition boxes inside → Each with its own file system

analogies.analogy: Like plugging a USB drive into a specific port - the port is the mount point, and the drive contents appear there after connection
Like dividing a large warehouse into separate rooms - each room (partition) can have different purposes (storage, office, inventory) and different rules
Like a universal remote that works with different TV brands - VFS is the universal interface hiding brand-specific (file system) details
